purpose: Characterize the Android emulator (emulator-5554, Pixel_API_35) virtual microphone to explain why acoustic ERLE is not measurable on the emulator, while the AEC transport/plumbing IS proven.

hostMicFloor_directSox.note: Mac built-in mic, 3s, sox -d — a clean, normal room floor (matches the accepted 9583-aec-macos loop's 0.015).

emulatorGuestMic_hostOutputMuted.note: Guest getUserMedia with the Mac output volume at 0. A faithful host-mic passthrough would read ~0.02 like the direct floor; instead it clips at full-scale with a near-DC zero-cross rate (not room tone, not the played far-end) — a synthetic emulator source.

emulatorGuestMic_duringHarnessRun.note: During the actual loop the near (mic) went near-silent while the far-end playback reference was delivered correctly (farRms 0.093). Either way the guest mic never carried the played-back acoustic echo.

conclusion: The Android emulator virtual microphone does not carry the host microphone (it is silent or a saturated synthetic source), so there is no real acoustic near-end to cancel and echo-only ERLE cannot be measured on the emulator. This is an emulator audio-I/O limitation (headless: no way to enable 'virtual mic uses host audio input' / grant the emulator macOS mic permission), NOT an app or AEC defect. What the emulator DOES prove is the whole on-device app path: the /api/voice/* transport, the live playback-reference producer (playbackSamplesReceived=417600, echoReferenceWired=true), the route-gate fix, and the native libelizainference.so load (#11372). Real acoustic ERLE comes from a physical device (iOS, once unlocked) and the accepted macOS loop (9583-aec-macos, ~68 ms, ERLE series).
